truth.papers.A27.title: Deformation behaviour of iron-rich iron-aluminum alloys at low temperatures
truth.papers.A27.paper_scope: The deformation behaviour of binary monocrystalline and polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys with Al contents up to 18 at.% and only low unavoidable impurity contents—in particular less than 100 wt.ppm C—has been studied at room temperature and −100 °C. The effects of quenching and annealing treatments on the behaviour of as-cast materials were investigated to clarify the dependence of strength and ductility on Al content and short-range ordering.

prediction.papers.A27.title: Deformation behaviour of iron-rich iron-aluminum alloys at low temperatures
prediction.papers.A27.paper_scope: Investigation of deformation behaviour of binary monocrystalline and polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys with Al up to 18 at.% and low carbon impurities (<100 wt.ppm C) at room temperature and -100°C, focusing on effects of quenching, annealing, Al content, and short-range ordering on strength and ductility.

truth.structures.idx_0.overall_structure: coarse grain structure with grain sizes of the order of 1 mm; single-phase ferritic Fe-Al solid solution, no second phases in as-cast condition

truth.structures.sample_fe12_poly_fc.microstru: cell walls with high dislocation density
truth.structures.sample_fe16_poly_q1100.overal: deformed polycrystalline Fe-16Al after quench and compression, showing glide bands in various directions with primarily straight dislocations between them
truth.structures.sample_fe16_poly_q1100.micros: polycrystalline with glide bands and straight dislocations
truth.structures.sample_fe16_poly_fc.overall_s: deformed polycrystalline Fe-16Al after furnace cooling and compression, leading to dislocation tangles between glide bands

truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.key_findi: Coarse grain structure with grain sizes of about 1 mm; as-cast alloys contained no second phases; thin platelike or rod-like precipitates observed on grain boundaries after slow furnace cooling or prolonged heat treatment at 320°C, believed to be carbides.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.technique: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray diffraction (EDX)
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.purpose: Fracture surface examination and qualitative precipitate analysis
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.key_findi: Monocrystals contained rare globular AlN inclusions up to 30 µm diameter; carbides were identified on grain boundaries in polycrystals.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_2.technique: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips CM20, 200 kV)
truth.characterization_methods.idx_2.key_findi: Fe-12%Al quenched: glide bands with dislocation tangles; furnace-cooled: cell formation. Fe-16%Al quenched: glide bands with straight dislocations; furnace-cooled: dislocation tangles.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_3.technique: Microhardness tester Fischerscope H100 (max load 1 N)
truth.characterization_methods.idx_3.key_findi: Hardness of Fe-16Al monocrystals varied only slightly with heat treatments, except for slight increase after quenching and slight decrease after long annealing.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_4.purpose: Measure yield stress at room temperature and -100°C at strain rates 10^-4 and 10^-2 s^-1
truth.characterization_methods.idx_4.key_findi: Yield stress increases linearly with Al content (hardening coefficient ~21-23 MPa/at.%); deformation twinning observed for Fe-18%Al at room temperature and for lower Al contents at -100°C.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_5.technique: Tensile testing according to DIN 50125 at rates 10^-4 to 10^-2 s^-1
truth.characterization_methods.idx_5.key_findi: Ductility decreases with increasing Al content; transition from ductile to brittle fracture at room temperature between 15-18% Al and at -100°C between 12-14% Al.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_fe12_pol: Real Al contents and impurity levels determined for studied alloys.
dislocation distribution after quenching and compression
Glide bands with dislocation tangles between them after 5.5% compression in quenched Fe-12%Al.
dislocation distribution after furnace cooling and compression
Cell formation after 5.5% compression in furnace-cooled Fe-12%Al.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_fe16_pol: dislocation distribution after quenching and compression
Glide bands in various directions with primarily straight dislocations between them after 5.5% compression in quenched Fe-16%Al.
dislocation distribution after furnace cooling and compression
Dislocation tangles between glide bands after 5.5% compression in furnace-cooled Fe-16%Al.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_fe16_mon: HU values for various heat treatments of Fe-16Al monocrystals; averages of more than 40 measurements.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Short-range ordering in Fe-Al alloys with Al contents 13-18% at temperatures below 600°C was found but has no major impact on deformation behaviour; only heat treatments above 600°C affect mechanical properties significantly.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Quenching from 1100°C produces excess vacancies that contribute to hardening; subsequent annealing at 300°C for 14 days only slightly reduces hardness, indicating slow kinetics of vacancy healing; vacancy formation enthalpy decreases with Al content and is about 1-1.2 eV for Fe-16-18%Al.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Deformation twinning occurs at −100°C for Al contents 11-18% and at room temperature only for 18% Al, attributed to reduced dislocation mobility; twinning is accompanied by audible clicks and large load drops.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Yield stress drop observed at −100°C for Fe-4%Al and Fe-6%Al, possibly related to excess vacancies and dislocation dynamics.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Ductile-to-brittle transition occurs between 15-18% Al at room temperature and 12-14% Al at −100°C; fracture without necking becomes dominant above critical Al levels.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Linear solid-solution hardening coefficient of 21 MPa per at.% Al at 10^-2 s^-1 and 23 MPa per at.% Al at 10^-4 s^-1 in compression at room temperature for polycrystalline as-cast alloys; hardening is well described by Suzuki’s theory for bcc alloys.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: The TEM micrographs in Figs. 6 and 7 reveal that quenching reduces dislocation mobility and affects the dislocation substructure (glide bands vs. cell formation); the pure text pipeline cannot read image details, but captions and discussion describe the contrast between quenched and furnace-cooled states.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Fig. 2 provides compressive stress-strain curves at room temperature (10^-4 s^-1) for [112] oriented Fe-16%Al monocrystals in as-grown, 600 °C quenched, and 300 °C/14d annealed states; stress values at 2-6% strain range from approximately 450 to 500 MPa depending on condition.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Fig. 4 presents tensile stress-strain curves at 10^-4 s^-1 for polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys at room temperature and -100 °C; Fe-18%Al shows coarse serrations due to deformation twinning at -100 °C, and Fe-4%Al exhibits a yield stress drop at -100 °C.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Fig. 5 provides tensile yield stress, ultimate tensile strength, strain before necking, and fracture strain at room temperature and -100 °C for as-cast polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys; exact numeric attribution of the tabulated Strength values in the caption to specific mechanical properties is ambiguous because of symbol rendering, though trends show linear yield stress increase with Al and ductility decrease.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Deformation twinning was observed in Fe-18%Al at room temperature under compression and for Al contents above about 11-14% at -100 °C under tension; twinning initiation stresses at -100 °C are plotted in Fig. 5(b).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: The compressive yield stress values in Fig. 3 (room temperature) are reported to equal the tensile yield stress for the same rate and temperature; values range from 50 MPa (pure Fe) to 470 MPa (Fe-18Al) with a hardening coefficient of about 2.2 GPa.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Short-range ordering occurs below 600 °C in Fe-Al alloys with 13-18% Al, but has no major impact on hardness or stress-strain behaviour; only heat treatments above 600 °C affect mechanical behaviour significantly through quenched-in excess vacancies.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Excess vacancies introduced by quenching from 1100 °C contribute to hardening and embrittlement; they heal out only slowly during annealing below 400 °C.
